title: Impact of digital screen media activity on functional brain organization in late childhood: Evidence from the ABCD study.
journal: Cortex; a journal devoted to the study of the nervous system and behavior
abstract: The idea that the increased ubiquity of digital devices negatively impacts neurodevelopment is as compelling as it is disturbing. This study investigated this concern by systematically evaluating how different profiles of screen-based engagement related to functional brain organization in late childhood. We studied participants from a large and representative sample of young people participating in the first two years of the ABCD study (ages 9-12 years) to investigate the relations between self-reported use of various digital screen media activity (SMA) and functional brain organization. A series of generalized additive mixed models evaluated how these relationships related to functional outcomes associated with health and cognition. Of principal interest were two hypotheses: First, that functional brain organization (assessed through resting state functional connectivity MRI; rs-fcMRI) is related to digital screen engagement; and second, that children with higher rates of engagement will have functional brain organization profiles related to maladaptive functioning. Results did not support either of these predictions for SMA. Further, exploratory analyses predicting how screen media activity impacted neural trajectories showed no significant impact of SMA on neural maturation over a two-year period.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 4.0, baseline sample of 11,878 participants ages 9-12 years. Paper states 7,309 participants with longitudinal functional MRI data at two timepoints.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures digital screen media activity (SMA) via self-reported 7-point scale assessing time spent on: TV, video/movie watching, video games, YouTube, digital device/computer games, texting/social media, and social media platforms. This is a specific smartphone/social media variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) used to test relationships between screen media activity profiles and functional brain organization (resting-state fMRI connectivity). Association tests reported: 'Results did not support either of these predictions for SMA' with exploratory analyses examining neural trajectories.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract states 'Results did not support either of these predictions for SMA. Further, exploratory analyses predicting how screen media activity impacted neural trajectories showed no significant impact of SMA on neural maturation over a two-year period.' Numeric statistics (test statistics, p-values, effect sizes) not visible in provided pages. Supplementary materials needed for verification.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Registered report design. Longitudinal study examining relationship between screen media activity and functional brain organization over two years with generalized additive mixed models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Cortex, 2023. Special Issue on ABCD study. Not a preprint or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is a predictor (exposure), and brain functional organization is the outcome. This is standard exposure→outcome directionality, not the FT-EC7 scenario.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study with participants aged 9-12 years from first two years of the study; ABCD Data Release 4.0 mentioned

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures self-reported digital screen media activity (SMA) including time spent on six digital activities: TV shows/movies, online videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat. Six-item 7-point scale for time spent per activity

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) testing relationships between screen media activity profiles and functional brain organization (resting state fMRI connectivity). Group factor analysis (GFA) with GAMM models examining correlations between digital screen engagement and brain network connectivity

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression statistics reported including factor loadings and p-values. GFA groupings explained variance (GFA grouping 1: 9.34% of variance; GFA grouping 2: 8.56% of total variance)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using registered report protocol, cross-sectional and exploratory longitudinal analyses of screen media activity and functional brain organization

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Cortex, 2023. Registered report. Not identified as duplicate

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is examined as a predictor of functional brain organization (brain connectivity), not as a dependent variable. Cross-sectional and exploratory longitudinal analyses are included. FT-EC7 does not apply.

title: Negative impact of daily screen use on inhibitory control network in preadolescence: A two-year follow-up study.
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has made an unprecedented shift in children's daily lives. Children are increasingly spending time with screens to learn and connect with others. As the online environment rapidly substitutes in-person experience, understanding children's neuropsychological trajectories associated with screen experiences is important. Previous findings suggest that excessive screen use can lead children to prefer more immediate rewards over delayed outcomes. We hypothesized that increased screen time delays a child's development of inhibitory control system in the brain (i.e., fronto-striatal circuitry). By analyzing neuropsychological data from 8324 children (9-11ys) from the ABCD Study, we found that children who had more screen time showed a higher reward orientation and weaker fronto-striatal connectivity. Importantly, we found that the daily screen exposure mediated the effect of reward sensitivity on the development of the inhibitory control system in the brain over a two year period. These findings suggest possible negative long-term impacts of increased daily screen time on children's neuropsychological development. The results further demonstrated that screen time influences dorsal striatum connectivity, which suggests that the effect of daily screen use is a habitual seeking behavior. The study provides neural and behavioral evidence for the negative impact of daily screen use on developing children.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD data: 8,324 children at baseline (ages 9-11 years, M=119.28 months) and 3,891 at 2-year follow-up from the ABCD 2.0 release

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time with Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) score computed from child self-report questionnaire of daily screen time across modalities

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation analyses, mediation analysis with bootstrapping, and group comparisons. Inferential statistics reported: baseline r = −0.040 (p < 0.001 by Bayes factor), year 2 r = −0.0933 (BF10 = 428995.74), group t-tests (t(1571) = 3.043, p = 0.002)

FT_IC4.evidence: Correlation coefficients, bootstrap 95% CIs, Bayes factors, p-values, Cohen's d, independent t-tests reported throughout (e.g., 'r = −0.0933, 95% CI [−0.1231, −0.0635]')

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using baseline and 2-year follow-up data examining screen time effects on fronto-striatal connectivity

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 2023

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria because screen time is the IV predicting neural outcomes (fronto-striatal connectivity), not the DV; includes individual psychological factors (reward sensitivity via BAS scores) as predictors; reports mediation analysis

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 8,324 children aged 9-11 years analyzed for neuropsychological associations

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'daily screen time' as specific exposure variable; abstract specifies screen time linked to inhibitory control, reward sensitivity, fronto-striatal connectivity

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports mediation analysis with statistical inference: 'screen time mediated the effect of reward sensitivity on development of inhibitory control system...over two year period' with neuropsychological outcomes (non-SM)

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract describes findings but does not provide explicit β, OR, p-values, or other numeric statistics. Full PDF text needed to confirm presence of numeric coefficients; cannot extract from abstract alone.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: mediation model predicting changes in fronto-striatal connectivity and inhibitory control over two-year period

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published 2023 in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience. No duplicate indication.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes non-SM outcomes (brain connectivity, inhibitory control development). Screen time is IV predicting neuropsychological outcomes, not DV. Does not meet criterion (a).

title: The longitudinal impact of screen media activities on brain function, architecture and mental health in early adolescence.
journal: International journal of clinical and health psychology : IJCHP
abstract: The increased use of screen media has raised unknown effects on mental health among adolescents. This study aimed to examine the correlational and causal association between screen media activity (SMA) and mental health problems, and the mediating role of brain functions and structures in this relationship. Data from 4557 adolescents (mean age = 9.955 Â± 0.164 years) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analysed across four time points: baseline, 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year follow-ups. Linear mixed models assessed SMA's association with mental health indices and the brain's developmental pattern, respectively. Cross-lagged panel models examined the SMA-mental health problems' longitudinal and causal relationship. Mediation analyses explored brain functions and structures as mediators on the SMA-mental health correlation. Baseline SMA positively correlated with internalizing, externalizing, and stress problems; and negatively correlated with brain volume, area and diverse sets of resting-state functional connectivity (RSFC) after three years. Higher baseline SMA associated with increased internalizing (Î² = 0.030, SE= 0.012, pfdr = 0.016), and stress problems (Î² = 0.026, SE = 0.012, pfdr = 0.037) three years later. The RSFC between the cingulo-opercular network (CON) and the retrosplenial temporal network (RTN) mediated the effects of SMA on externalizing (Î² = 0.002, pfdr = 0.042) and stress problems (Î² = -0.003, pfdr = 0.022). TV watching predicted higher externalizing problems (Î² = 0.054, pfdr < 0.001), while video watching predicted increased internalizing (Î² = 0.061, pfdr < 0.001), externalizing (Î² = 0.033, pfdr = 0.035), and stress problems (Î² = 0.060, pfdr < 0.001). The findings indicate the negative impact of SMA, particularly TV and video watching, on adolescent mental health, mediated by changes in CON and RTN functional connectivity. Future research can explore the specific risks associated with video streaming and consider the role of emerging technologies such as virtual reality in SMA on adolescent mental health.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from 4,557 adolescents (mean age 9.955±0.164 years) across four time points: baseline, 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year follow-ups.

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is 'screen media activity (SMA)' defined as six types: TV watching, video watching, video games, texting, social media, and video chat. However, the paper reports results as 'TV watching,' 'video watching,' and 'video games' separately, not as a single smartphone/social media variable. TV and video games do not qualify. Text states 'TV watching predicted higher externalizing problems' and 'video watching predicted increased internalizing' and 'video games.' While social media is measured, the primary results emphasize TV and video (non-phone video watching). No single extractable smartphone/SM-specific coefficient. Mixed-modality paper with only aggregate or non-SM results emphasized.

FT_IC3.evidence: Because FT-IC2 is not met for a distinct smartphone/SM variable, FT-IC3 cannot be met. Although the paper reports linear mixed models and cross-lagged panel models with statistical tests, these are on TV, video, and video games—not on smartphone or social media-specific measures as standalone exposures.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values are reported (β estimates with p-values), but only for TV watching, video watching, and video games, not for a smartphone/social media-specific association.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed models, cross-lagged panel models, and mediation analyses examining screen media activities' association with mental health outcomes and brain development across multiple time points.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Clinical and Health Psychology, 2025. No evidence of duplication.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 not met; paper fails on smartphone/social media exposure criterion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study data: N=4557 adolescents (mean age 9.955±0.164 years) analyzed across four time points: baseline, 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year follow-ups (2017-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'Screen media activity (SMA)' with six component types: television, videos, video games, video chat, social media. Social media component is separately measured and analyzed. Paper explicitly states measurement includes 'visiting social networking sites' and reports SMA-specific and media-specific breakdowns. Video watching and social media coefficients are separately extractable

FT_IC3.evidence: Uses linear mixed models, cross-lagged panel models, and mediation analyses. Reports inferential tests linking SMA to mental health outcomes (internalizing, externalizing, stress problems) and brain developmental patterns. Example: 'Higher baseline SMA associated with increased internalizing (β = 0.030, SE = 0.012, pfdr = 0.016)' at 3-year follow-up. Also tests direct/indirect effects through brain functional connectivity (RSFC) mediation

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β coefficients with SE and p-values throughout. Table 2 reports direct effects: Externalizing/CON-RTN β=0.679, p=0.0004; Total SMA/CON-hippocampus β=0.036, p=0.0198. Text reports: 'Higher baseline SMA associated with increased internalizing (β = 0.030, SE = 0.012, pfdr = 0.016)' and mediation effects with β and p-values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal study with multiple analytic approaches: linear mixed models assessing SMA associations with mental health indices and brain developmental patterns; cross-lagged panel models examining SMA-mental health causality; mediation analyses exploring brain functions/structures as mediators

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Clinical and Health Psychology (2025), original research article (not preprint, duplicate, or protocol)

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports SMA as independent variable predicting mental health outcomes (forward direction), with brain structural/functional features (neural connectivity) as mediators. Includes individual neural predictors (RSFC, cortical morphology) as mechanisms—not demographic-only predictors. Does not meet EC7 criteria
